query: Main Gate Syndrome definition and its relevance in mass-casualty trauma care

retrieved_docs.title: Duty to Warn -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: This concept also encompasses patients visiting the emergency department. [17] In the emergency department, most threats are made under the influence of drugs, alcohol, or anger. Emergency physicians often encounter patients for the first time, so it is difficult to assess the gravity of their threats and foresee the victims. [18] Duty to warn and duty to report are integral domains of the Ethics checklist in research with ethical, legal, and social implications. [19] The Ratzan criteria for determining the suitability of sharing an unsolicited observation about bystanders include: A high probability of a serious disease A high likelihood that the individual is unaware of the condition Adequate knowledge of the disease The urgency of the diagnosis Provision of a private conversation [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Emergency Preparedness -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Emergency preparedness encompasses the planning and response to disasters. A disaster is defined by the World Health Organization (WHO) as a sudden phenomenon of sufficient magnitude to overwhelm the resources of a hospital, region, or location requiring external support. [1] Based on this definition, a disaster could be as massive as the terrorist attacks of September 11, 2001, or as small as a single patient with an infectious disease. Defining a disaster ultimately depends on the type of event, timing, the severity of illness or injuries, local preparedness, and resources available. Disasters are either internal or external disasters. Internal disasters are events that occur within the walls of the hospital itself, such as an active shooter, power outage, or radiation exposure. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Zones of Care -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Following the 2012 Sandy Hook massacre, national experts gathered in 2013 to improve survival during mass casualty shootings. This initiative led to the Hartford Consensus, a strategic framework spearheaded by the American College of Surgeons Committee on Trauma. [7] The original Hartford Consensus document introduced the acronym THREAT, emphasizing coordinated action through training, policy development, and public education. This acronym is defined as follows: Threat suppression Hemorrhage control Rapid extraction to safety Assessment by medical providers Transport to definitive care [8] [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Vascular Extremity Trauma -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: As with all trauma patients, advanced trauma life support (ATLS) protocol should be followed as with any patient that presents to the trauma bay.  After completion of airway and breathing evaluation, assessment of circulation is next.  The management of vascular injuries has its basis the concept of hard or soft signs.  The WTA and EAST each have different definitions of hard signs and soft signs.  The WTA defines hard signs as expanding hematoma, bruit, thrill, external bleeding, pulselessness, pallor, paresthesia, paralysis or pain. The WTA recommends using the hard signs for major named arteries, which includes anything proximal to the anterior tibial artery or tibioperoneal bifurcation but it excludes the profunda femoris artery.  The EAST defines hard signs as pulsatile bleeding, thrill, pulse deficit, bruit or expanding hematoma.  The definition of soft signs, per the WTA, is a history of arterial bleeding at the scene or in transit, neurological deficit occurring in a nerve adjacent to a name artery, proximity of the injury to name artery or a small nonpulsatile hematoma present over an artery.  The EAST definition of soft signs is the same as the WTA. [1] [10] The finding of a single soft sign has approximately a 10% rate of vascular injury.  In comparison, two or more soft signs can have a vascular injury rate of 25%. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Criteria For Disaster Declaration -- Introduction -- After the Disaster
retrieved_docs.content: Once a disaster has occurred and it is apparent that the area’s recovery needs outweigh the scope of local resources, the governor will contact their local FEMA office to request a joint federal-state and tribal preliminary damage assessment (PDA). This assessment should outline how and why the provincial government resources are inadequate to address the local disaster. In conjunction with local representatives, the federal team assesses the affected area to determine the extent of the disaster, who and what public resources are affected, and what federal Assistance will be needed. The request for disaster declaration must be made through the local FEMA office within 30 days of the disaster.

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Management Of Traumatic And Medical Disorders In A Wilderness Environment -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Wilderness medicine is an amalgamation of many different medical disciplines. The base medical knowledge needed for competent practice aligns closely with emergency medicine and primary care specialties. However, familiarity with many other subspecialties, such as emergency medical services (EMS), expedition medicine, tactical medicine, travel medicine, and others, may be necessary depending on individual practice circumstances. With such a broad knowledge base, an agreed-upon definition of wilderness medicine has proven difficult. Most reasonable definitions center on delayed time to definitive care and limitations of available resources.

retrieved_docs.title: Emergency Department Triage -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Triage originates from the French word "trier," which is used to describe the processes of sorting and organization. Triage is utilized in the healthcare community to categorize patients based on the severity of their injuries and, by extension, the order in which multiple patients require care and monitoring. The history of the emergency triage originated in the military for field doctors. As early as the 18th century, documentation shows how field surgeons would quickly look over soldiers and determine if there was anything they could do for the wounded soldier. French military surgeon Baron Dominique Jean Larrey, the chief surgeon in Napoleon Bonaparte's imperial guard, developed a system based on the need to evaluate and categorize wounded soldiers quickly during battle. The triage system was first implemented in hospitals in 1964 when Weinerman et al. published a systematic interpretation of civilian emergency departments using triage. Today, triage is still deeply integrated into healthcare. Triage can be broken down into three phases: prehospital triage, triage at the scene of the event, and triage upon arrival to the emergency department. There are various triage systems implemented around the world, but the universal goal of triage is to supply effective and prioritized care to patients while optimizing resource usage and timing. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Mass Casualty Management -- Clinical Significance -- Inventory
retrieved_docs.content: Inventory of resources is as paramount as resource allocation. Inventory methods should be adaptable and scalable. Inventory lists can be created on paper or electronic spreadsheets on the scene or in the hospital setting. The Incident Resource Inventory System provided by the Federal Emergency Management Agency at no cost is a standards-based information software tool that “allows users to identify & inventory their resources, consistently with National Incident Management System resource typing definitions, for mutual aid operations based on mission needs and each resource’s capabilities, availability and response time, and share information with other agencies.”

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Criteria For Disaster Declaration -- Introduction -- Types of Disaster Declarations
retrieved_docs.content: Emergency Declarations are employed for any situation where federal Assistance is needed. Assistance may not exceed $5 million unless the president seeks additional approval by Congress. State or Tribal governments may request a pre-disaster declaration when there are imminently anticipated needs before the event. Under this type of declaration, the federal government may provide limited resources. There are 2 types of Assistance under this program: Public and Individual. States or Tribal Governments are not prohibited from seeking a Major Disaster Declaration if the needs of the locale exceed the resources provided by the Emergency Declaration. For an Emergency Declaration, the governor must describe other federal agency resources already utilized and how much additional federal Assistance is required.

retrieved_docs.title: Emergency Department Triage -- Definition/Introduction -- Field and Disaster Triage
retrieved_docs.content: Emergency medicine services (EMS) are the front-line personnel that are the first eyes and ears on patients. Their clinical decision making is just as important as physicians when it comes to the outcome of a patient. In mass casualty events or disasters, the emergency providers must be able to quickly size up the scene, develop an action plan, and do the most good for the most amount of people. These can include difficult decisions being made by physicians, EMS, and nurses regarding who to provide care for immediately, who can wait, and who cannot be saved. There have been many different algorithms in how to properly triage patients in the field to help responders develop a system on care. One of these algorithms is called START triage, which stands for "simple triage and rapid transport." As emergency responders arrive at the scene, victims are asked to walk to a designated area marked off for care. Anyone who can follow these commands and walk to this area is designated as "minor" and given a green tag to signify minor injury status. Once the "minor" injuries are out of the area, responders should begin to move and triage patients with the RPM acronym; respirations, perfusion, and mental status. This includes making sure the individual has a manual respiration rate that is roughly greater than 30 breaths a minute, peripheral pulses are present with a capillary refill of fewer than 2 seconds and can follow commands. If a patient has none of these, the patient is declared deceased, given a black tag, and moved to the black coded area. If individuals can breathe spontaneously, follow simple commands, and have distal pulses with a normal capillary refill, they are tagged delayed and given the code yellow. The rest of the individuals who have poor respirations or cannot protect their airway, have absent or decreased peripheral pulses, and unable to follow simple commands are tagged immediately and given the color red. With this method, providers can quickly rule in and rule out individuals who require immediate medical attention, who can wait, and who nothing can be done for. However, the assignment of individuals in this algorithm is purely based on vital signs that can change rapidly in the field.

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Tactical Paramedic Lethal Triad -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Traumatic injuries unleash a cascade of factors contributing to patient peril, warranting thorough examination. This topic explains the lethal triad comprising hypothermia, acidosis, and coagulopathy, pivotal indicators of injury severity and influential determinants of patient fate. These conditions herald heightened mortality rates, highlighting the intricate interplay shaping patient outcomes. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: EMS System Regionalization -- Issues of Concern -- Outcomes in Regionalized Systems
retrieved_docs.content: Severe trauma:  Time to surgical intervention for hemorrhage control is critical for trauma management, and regionalization of trauma care has greatly improved survival. Trauma systems typically include a network of designated trauma centers and an EMS system that directs patients meeting field trauma triage criteria to these specialized hospitals. Research has shown that patients treated at trauma centers have better outcomes compared to those treated elsewhere. [11] [17] [18] [19] [20] [21] For example, one study found that patients treated within a trauma system had a reduced preventable mortality from 30% to 16% [4] compared to those who did not receive regionalized trauma care. Another study found that adding a second helicopter, which reduced transport times and allowed for effective regionalization into a county-wide trauma system on Long Island, improved mortality by 5%. [22] Trauma systems provide a framework for communication and coordination between different parts of the health system to decrease delays and barriers to emergency surgery and other definitive management.

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Mass Casualty Response -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Mass casualty incidents (MCIs) are disasters, either man-made or natural, in which local management agencies and the healthcare system are overwhelmed. Emergency medical services (EMS) provides a vital role in triaging, stabilizing, and transporting victims to definitive care during MCIs.  There may be large numbers of victims who will need care. Strategies and plans for effective management, communication, and collaboration are essential during an MCI. The military, fire department, police department, search, and rescue units, as well as the country's or jurisdiction's health ministry, may become involved depending on the type and severity of an MCI.

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Termination Of Resuscitation And Pronouncement of Death -- Clinical Significance -- Resuscitation in Trauma
retrieved_docs.content: While the TOR criteria for OHCA in nontraumatic patients have been studied extensively, TOR research in trauma patients has been scarce. However, many causes of OHCA in trauma patients are due to acute blood loss and traumatic injuries that may be repaired with prompt surgical intervention. The NAEMSP-ASCOT 2012 update suggested that TOR protocols may be appropriate for EMS agencies. [30] The TOR rules are predicated on EMS providers' ability to get patients to definitive care in a reasonable amount of time. This would take precedence over any on-scene measures due to the likely need for surgical intervention. EMS agencies must coordinate with their local trauma centers and assess their resources to develop appropriate TOR rules for their community. More research is necessary before the widespread implementation of standard TOR rules in trauma patients.

retrieved_docs.title: EMS: Trauma Center Designation -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The American College of Surgeons (ACS) evaluates and verifies trauma centers to improve trauma care. Unlike designation, ACS verification confirms that a facility meets resource and operational standards for patients with traumatic injuries. The ACS assesses a center’s preparedness, policies, resources, and quality improvement processes, granting verification for 3 years. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Mass Casualty Triage -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The World Health Organization defines mass casualty incidents as disasters and major incidents characterized by quantity, severity, and diversity of patients that can rapidly overwhelm the ability of local medical resources to deliver comprehensive and definitive medical care. They have been occurring more frequently in recent decades and affect countries of all socioeconomic backgrounds. Preparedness and planning are vital, as these events can happen in any community at any given time. Defined pre-hospital triage systems are essential in saving lives and optimizing the initiation of resource allocation when these disasters strike. [1] [2] [3] [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2463
retrieved_docs.content: Unspecified site injury is a broad injury category that encompasses more specific conditions such as intracranial injury, indicating that injuries without a clearly defined location may include trauma to the brain. This category is primarily derived from the concept of multiple injuries (إصابات متعددة), reflecting that when several injuries occur together or the exact site is not specified, they are often grouped under a general “unspecified site” label. Recognizing that unspecified site injury can represent both intracranial trauma and a collection of multiple injuries helps clinicians adopt a comprehensive assessment approach, ensuring that potential brain involvement is not overlooked and that the overall injury burden is appropriately addressed in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2623
retrieved_docs.content: The phrase “Due to” is associated with crushing injury or internal injury, and crushing injury or internal injury is in turn associated with the Arabic term “إصابة,” which means injury; thus, when a condition is described as “due to” a crushing or internal injury, it denotes a direct causal link to an injury.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1715
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebral injury due to birth trauma is recognized as a specific manifestation of birth trauma, indicating that this type of brain injury occurs as a result of traumatic events during delivery. In medical terminology, the concept of birth trauma corresponds to the Arabic phrase “نزيف مخي وليدي,” linking the English description of traumatic birth‑related injuries to its equivalent expression in Arabic. This relationship underscores that cerebral injury caused by birth trauma is a subset of the broader category of birth trauma and that the term is directly mapped to its Arabic counterpart for cross‑linguistic clinical reference.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2454
retrieved_docs.content: FACE INJURY TRAUMA and chest injury trauma are medical conditions that frequently co‑occur with حادثة سير (traffic accident). In patients involved in a traffic accident, clinicians should be alert to the high likelihood of both facial and chest injuries, as these trauma types commonly arise together in the context of such incidents. Recognizing this association aids in comprehensive assessment, timely diagnosis, and appropriate management of trauma patients following a traffic accident.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2187
retrieved_docs.content: Accident caused by firearm missile is identified as a specific type of accidental physical contact event and is directly associated with injury by a bullet (إصابة بعيار ناري). This classification highlights that firearm missile accidents represent a subset of accidental physical contact traumas, with the principal clinical manifestation being a bullet wound, which is essential for accurate injury coding, epidemiologic tracking, and appropriate emergency medical management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 821
retrieved_docs.content: In medical terminology, the concept “burns” is closely linked to various types of injury. The relationship labeled “associated_with” connects “burns” to the Arabic term “جرح حروق” (burn wound), indicating that burn wounds are a primary manifestation of burns. Additionally, “burns” maintains a “has relationship” link with “حرق حراري” (thermal burn), specifying that exposure to heat is a common cause of burn injuries. The hierarchical notion expressed by “has child” ties the term “جرح حروق” to “حرق من سائل” (liquid burn), denoting that liquid burns are a subcategory or child type of burn wound. Together, these connections illustrate that burn injuries encompass thermal and liquid mechanisms, with burn wounds serving as the clinical presentation, and the relational descriptors “has child,” “has relationship,” and “associated_with” clarifying the structural and causal links among these concepts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 784
retrieved_docs.content: Complication is linked to multiple open wounds that have not yet resulted in additional problems, and it is also associated with open wounds of the extremities. This relationship underscores that even when open wounds—particularly numerous ones or those located on the limbs—have not produced further complications, they are still considered relevant to the broader concept of complication in clinical assessment and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2559
retrieved_docs.content: The phrase “Due to” is associated with crushing injury or internal injury, indicating that these types of injuries are frequently described as occurring because of specific causative events. This association highlights the importance of identifying the underlying mechanisms when evaluating patients with crushing or internal injuries, as the wording “due to” often signals a direct link between the injury and its precipitating factor.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1169
retrieved_docs.content: Burn of head AND/OR neck is recognized as a specific, narrower form of injury within the broader category of Burns and corrosions. The Arabic term جرح حروق refers to burn wounds and is directly linked to the concept of burns; burns are associated with جرح حروق. Consequently, the head and neck burn subtype falls under the general classification of Burns and corrosions and is connected to the Arabic designation for burn injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2666
retrieved_docs.content: In medical terminology, a complication can be associated with the presence of multiple open wounds that themselves are not complicated, indicating that a broader clinical complication may arise even when each individual wound does not exhibit additional problems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2027
retrieved_docs.content: Open wounds (جرح مفتوح) are clinically associated with crushing injuries or internal injuries, indicating that such traumatic mechanisms often result in an open wound presentation. Within the category of open wounds, cut wounds (جرح قطع) represent a specific subtype, meaning that a cut wound is a particular form of open wound. Recognizing that crushing or internal injuries can lead to open wounds, and that cut wounds are a distinct subset of these, aids clinicians in diagnosing the nature of the trauma and selecting appropriate management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2514
retrieved_docs.content: External cause codes: Firearm are classified as injuries due to legal intervention by firearm, indicating that when a firearm is used in a legally sanctioned action that results in injury, the appropriate external cause code reflects this specific category. This classification helps clinicians, medical coders, and public health officials accurately document and analyze firearm‑related injuries occurring under legal authority, thereby supporting injury surveillance, resource allocation, and policy development.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 1057
retrieved_docs.content: grouped under a unified late‑effect classification that is further subdivided by anatomical region. This structured relationship aids clinicians in linking acute injury diagnoses with their potential long‑term sequelae and facilitates comprehensive patient management across different injury types and body regions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1627
retrieved_docs.content: General injuries represent a category of traumatic abnormality that serves as a parent concept for more specific injury types. Within this broader classification, the narrower subcategories include chest injury (إصابة الصدر) and skin injury (إصابة في الجلد). By organizing these specific injuries as child entities of general injuries, clinicians can more precisely identify, document, and manage the particular anatomical locations and characteristics of traumatic events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 1186
retrieved_docs.content: Internal injury of chest is categorized as a type of crushing injury or internal injury, indicating that it has the broader parent classification of crushing injury or internal injury. As a traumatic event, an internal injury of chest can act as a cause of subsequent medical complications. The occurrence of this chest injury is frequently described as being “due to” and therefore is associated with crushing mechanisms or other internal injury processes. Understanding that internal injury of chest is both a traumatic event that can be a cause of further injury and a subcategory within crushing injury or internal injury helps clinicians assess its etiology, anticipate related complications, and guide appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 193
retrieved_docs.content: Antemortem injury is mapped to the category “Unspecified site injury,” which is primarily derived from “multiple injuries” (إصابات متعددة) and serves as a broad grouping that includes intracranial injury. Intracranial injury is classified as “إصابة داخل الجمجمة” and encompasses specific head‑trauma entities such as Fracture of anterior fossa, Fracture of middle fossa, and “إصابة الرأس المغلقة,” all of which are instances of “إصابة داخل الجمجمة.” The concept “إصابة داخل الجمجمة” is linked to the generic notion of wound (جرح). Burn of head and/or neck is related to “إصابة داخل الجمجمة” and is placed under the narrower subset of Burns and corrosions. The contextual group “Circumstances Related to Injury or Death” includes the subcategories assault (الاعتداء) and self‑inflicted injury (تشويه الذات); “تشويه الذات” is also associated with wound (جرح). Closed fracture of the vault of the skull with cerebral laceration and/or contusion is classified both as a child of “كسر في الجمجمة” and as a child of intracranial injury, reinforcing its placement within the intracranial injury domain. The term “إصابة حادة في الدماغ” is considered synonymous with “إصابة داخل الجمجمة” and, through an inverse‑isa relationship, is related to brain pathology (اعتلال دماغي). A miscellaneous injury category titled “إصابات، غير مصنف في موضع آخر الموضع، غير مصنف في موضع آخر” contains children such as muscle and bone injury (إصابة العضلات والعظام) and wound (جرح), illustrating the hierarchical organization of injury types for comprehensive medical documentation and patient care. Hereditary syndrome (مُتَلاَزِمَةٌ هَرْسِيَّة) includes, via a “has child” relationship, the sub‑category “إصابات، غير مصنف في موضع آخر الموضع، غير مصنف في موضع آخر,” indicating that patients with hereditary syndromes may present with unclassified injuries that must be considered during evaluation and management.
